_format_scope: composition + arrangement + performance + stylistic_figures. Excludes mixing/mastering/recording — user handles those via Mix Analyzer on the produced track.
_reviewer_notes: Album/year (Songs for the Deaf, 2002) and runtime ~3:19 confirmed against track listing. Tempo 126-132 BPM plausible (~129 commonly cited mid-tempo). Drop D tuning is the documented Songs for the Deaf convention. Dave Grohl as guest drummer for the entire album confirmed. Mark Lanegan harmony contributions across multiple Songs for the Deaf tracks documented; track-specific role appropriately hedged in _to_verify_fields. Robot-rock micro-variation framing matches Homme's documented compositional philosophy. No factual corrections needed.
_high_confidence_fields: Dave Grohl on drums (documented Songs for the Deaf album drummer)
Mark Lanegan harmony vocal contribution (documented across multiple Songs for the Deaf tracks)
Homme's documented robot-rock compositional philosophy (repetitive hypnotic riff with micro-variations)
_to_verify_fields: exact key area F-sharp minor vs G-sharp minor reading
exact bar counts of pre-chorus / bridge
specific guitar amp choices for this track within Homme's documented palette
exact role of Lanegan harmony per section
time_signature: 4/4 throughout — danceable mid-tempo rock pulse. No metric tricks; the energy comes from Homme's robot-rock groove philosophy applied within standard 4/4.
key_area: F-sharp minor or G-sharp minor area (Homme's documented baritone-friendly tonal area on Songs for the Deaf). Tonic minor with riff motion toward relative major area in chorus. Voice-leading desert-rock-craft with Black Sabbath / Kyuss lineage influence.
tuning_documented: Drop D tuning (documented Homme/QOTSA convention) — adds chromatic riff capability via low D string + low-end weight. Bass also Drop D for matching tuning convention.

composition.structural_blueprint: riff_intro_immediate (Homme guitar + Grohl drums + Oliveri bass enter together in tight power-trio statement, no gradual build) -> verse1_with_homme_baritone_vocal (riff continues, Homme's baritone melodic vocal enters, micro-variations on the riff create breathing groove) -> pre_chorus_density_build (slight density build + harmonic shift, Grohl fill leading into chorus) -> chorus1_with_lanegan_harmony (hook expansion, Mark Lanegan whiskey-baritone harmony enters above/below Homme, density expands but stays power-trio clean) -> verse2 (returns to verse density) -> pre_chorus2 -> chorus2 (slight intensification, Lanegan harmony continues) -> bridge_brief_textural_shift (possibly clean-guitar break or harmonic suspension before final chorus) -> final_chorus_with_riff_outro (final chorus + riff statement to close)

section_count_and_lengths.intro: Brief — 4-8 bars of riff before vocal entry
section_count_and_lengths._note: Bar counts approximate. Total runtime 3:19 = tight commercial-single economy by QOTSA standards but with Homme's signature hypnotic groove foundation. Compositional movement compact yet richly textured.

composition.harmonic_motion: Minor-modal with characteristic Homme pentatonic-influenced riff motion. Tonic F#/G# minor with chord shifts to relative major area in chorus. Voice-leading desert-rock-craft (documented Black Sabbath, Kyuss, Queens-era Sabbath stoner-rock lineage influence). Verse riff cycles a small chord set; chorus opens harmonic palette slightly.
composition.modal_choice: Minor (Aeolian) with pentatonic-based riff vocabulary characteristic of stoner-rock / desert-rock lineage. Less Phrygian-tinged than NIN material; more straightforward minor pentatonic with chromatic ornamentation.
composition.melodic_contour: Homme's vocal contour is BARITONE-MELODIC SUSTAINED — phrases trace melodic shapes within his baritone-friendly range, sustained note durations rather than rapid declamation. Less aggressive than typical rock-vocal contour. Chorus contour opens with melodic lift; Lanegan's whiskey-baritone harmony adds counter-contour above or below Homme's primary line.
composition.characteristic_riff_construction: REPETITIVE HYPNOTIC RIFF with MICRO-VARIATIONS — Homme's signature 'robot rock' construction. Riff repeats near-identically across measures but never EXACTLY identically: slight timing shifts, occasional different pickup notes, subtle phrasing variations create hypnotic groove that breathes. Built on Drop D tuning for low chromatic riff capability. Riff is playable + memorable but not technically demanding — groove over complexity.
composition.harmonic_pacing: Slow at the chord level (modal-static verses with limited chord motion) but HYPNOTIC at the riff level (riff micro-variations create perceived motion within chord stability). Chorus harmonic pacing opens slightly with relative-major movement; bridge may introduce brief harmonic suspension.
composition.phrase_symmetry: Symmetric 4-bar and 8-bar phrasing throughout. Standard verse-pre-chorus-chorus structure. Symmetry serves accessibility; Homme uses asymmetry sparingly. The hypnotic feel comes from riff micro-variation within symmetric phrase structure, not from asymmetric meter.

instrumentation_complete: Lead electric guitar (Josh Homme — Drop D tuned, tube amps from Marshall/Hiwatt/Mesa-Boogie palette)
Possible double-tracked rhythm guitar (Homme documented as often layering rhythm guitars for stereo width)
Slight modulation effects on guitars (chorus, occasional flange) for desert-rock textural color
Electric bass (Nick Oliveri — Drop D tuned, pick attack, hardcore-punk-influenced approach)
Drums (Dave Grohl — guest drummer on Songs for the Deaf entire album, full kit)
Lead vocal (Josh Homme — baritone melodic delivery)
Harmony vocal (Mark Lanegan — whiskey-baritone harmony, documented contributor across multiple Songs for the Deaf tracks)

section_instrumentation.intro: Power-trio immediate entry — Homme guitar + Grohl drums + Oliveri bass in tight unison. No gradual build, no atmospheric pad.
section_instrumentation.verse: Riff continues, Homme baritone vocal enters, Oliveri bass tight with Grohl kick, possible additional guitar overdub layer subtly behind
section_instrumentation.pre_chorus: Slight density build + harmonic shift, Grohl fill leading into chorus
section_instrumentation.chorus: Hook expansion, Mark Lanegan harmony enters above/below Homme, chorus density expands but stays POWER-TRIO clean — no wall-of-sound layering
section_instrumentation.bridge: Brief textural/dynamic shift — possibly clean-guitar break or harmonic suspension
section_instrumentation.outro: Final chorus + riff statement to close — abrupt punk-style ending or short fade

arrangement.vocal_layering_strategy: Power-trio-plus-guest aesthetic. Homme primary lead vocal; Lanegan adds whiskey-baritone harmony as DIMENSIONAL COLOR, not as wall-of-sound stacking. Lanegan placed slightly behind Homme + slightly wider for dimensional placement. Different vocal identity = different emotional color (Lanegan's documented Screaming Trees + solo material vocal lineage informs his contribution). Less vocal layering than wall-of-sound bands — every layer has distinct identity and reason.
arrangement.dynamic_arc_overall: Sustained mid-tempo groove with moderate dynamic arc — verse-chorus density expansion, pre-chorus build, brief bridge dip, final chorus payoff. Less sawtooth than NIN industrial tracks; more hypnotic-cumulative than rock-anthem-explosive. The track feels DRIVING throughout rather than dynamic-shifting.

instrumental_role_assignment.guitar_role: REPETITIVE HYPNOTIC RIFF as groove foundation + desert-rock textural color. Homme's documented robot-rock guitar approach: groove-foundation-oriented, riff + groove priority over melodic-flash or texture-aggression.
instrumental_role_assignment.bass_role: Tight groove-lock with Grohl's kick + aggressive pick attack. Oliveri's documented punk-influenced approach within QOTSA's stoner-rock framework. Slight melodic activity between riff sections, but groove-foundation primary.
instrumental_role_assignment.drums_role: Groove-conscious power-trio drumming. Grohl as guest brings technical-precision + rock-power but ADAPTS to Homme's groove-priority philosophy. More technical precision than Nirvana drumming — guest-drummer mode for Songs for the Deaf. Plays FOR the song, not for the spotlight.
instrumental_role_assignment.vocal_role: Homme baritone melodic lead carries the song; Lanegan harmony adds dimensional color in chorus. Two vocal identities, both intact, complementary rather than redundant.

harmonic_density_per_section.intro: Medium — power-trio entry establishes density immediately, no gradual build
harmonic_density_per_section.verse: Medium — power-trio + vocal, possibly slight overdub layer
harmonic_density_per_section.pre_chorus: Medium-high — density builds via instrumentation lift
harmonic_density_per_section.chorus: High — power-trio + Homme vocal + Lanegan harmony, but still tighter than wall-of-sound (every layer earns its place)
harmonic_density_per_section.final_chorus: High — full chorus density + possible additional emphasis

arrangement_anti_patterns_avoided: No drum sample replacement (Grohl's drums are essential)
No atmospheric synth pads (power-trio aesthetic priority)

performance.tempo_feel_description: Mid-tempo hypnotic groove at sustained ~129 BPM. Feels both DRIVING (sustained rock motion) and POCKET (deep groove pocket from Grohl/Oliveri lock). Tempo enables danceable mid-tempo rock without rushing. The feel is loose-but-tight: micro-variations in riff phrasing create breathing while drum/bass lock keeps it rock-solid.

drum_style.performer: Dave Grohl (guest drummer on Songs for the Deaf — documented as drumming on entire album)
drum_style.feel: Groove-conscious power-trio drumming. Grohl restrains his Nirvana-era aggression and brings groove-conscious playing for Homme's robot-rock context. More technical precision than Nirvana drumming (guest-drummer mode).
techniques_documented: Kick + snare backbone driving 4/4 at ~129 BPM with Grohl's documented power
Hi-hat 8ths driving forward with tight groove pocket
Snare placement strong on 2+4 with possible occasional ghost notes for groove feel
Fills technically dense but groove-conscious — Grohl adapts vocabulary to QOTSA's groove-priority context
Cymbal accents articulate chorus moments + transitions
Pre-chorus fill is the signature drum gesture — tight, groove-preserving, lifting into the chorus
drum_style.groove_feel_alignment: Tight groove-lock with Oliveri's bass for power-trio pocket. Listeners feel the kick-bass coordination as a single rhythmic event. Grohl's drumming personality preserved within Homme's compositional context.
drum_style.fills_and_transitions: Pre-chorus fills mark the verse-to-chorus transitions as the primary fill moment. Less fills overall than rock-band-spotlight drumming — Grohl plays FOR the song. Bridge transition fills are briefer than chorus-entry fills.

feel_per_section.intro_riff: Riff established immediately with full power-trio entry; Drop D tuning provides low-end weight
feel_per_section.verse: Repetitive hypnotic riff with micro-variations — riff repeats near-identically with slight timing shifts and occasional phrasing variations creating hypnotic groove
feel_per_section.pre_chorus: Density build via slight texture addition
feel_per_section.chorus: Chord-emphasized chorus guitars opening harmonic palette to relative major area
feel_per_section.bridge_solo: No traditional guitar solo (groove-priority context). Bridge may feature clean-guitar break or sustained chord rather than virtuosic display.

techniques_documented: Drop D tuning (documented Homme/QOTSA convention) for low-end weight + chromatic riff capability
Possible double-tracked rhythm guitars (Homme's documented layering preference)
Tube amps (Marshall, Hiwatt, Mesa-Boogie palette per documented Homme rig)
Distortion + slight modulation (chorus, occasional flange) for desert-rock textural color
Picking precision required for hypnotic-repetition micro-variation feel
Stereo placement: rhythm guitars panned hard L/R for power-trio stereo width
guitar_style.playing_style_versus_other_grunge: Homme guitars are GROOVE-FOUNDATION-ORIENTED — riff + groove priority over melodic-flash or texture-aggression. Where Cobain plays minimal-melodic, Cornell plays melodic-intensity, Corgan plays layered-density, Reznor plays texture-aggression, Homme plays GROOVE-FOUNDATION. Distinct from grunge guitar paradigms in deployment intent.

vocal_delivery.performer: Josh Homme (lead vocal) + Mark Lanegan (harmony/secondary vocal — documented contributor across multiple Songs for the Deaf tracks)
vocal_delivery.verse_delivery: Homme baritone melodic — sustained melodic phrasing in his documented baritone-friendly tonal area, less aggressive than typical rock-vocal, restrained but engaged
vocal_delivery.chorus_delivery: Homme melodic lift + Lanegan whiskey-baritone harmony — Lanegan adds dimensional vocal color above/below Homme's primary line
vocal_delivery.outro_delivery: Possibly final hook delivery + riff outro, possibly Lanegan harmony continuing or fading
documented_vocal_quirks: Homme's documented baritone-friendly compositional choices — his tonal area shapes the songs
Lanegan harmony contribution documented as bringing whiskey-baritone color via his Screaming Trees + solo material vocal lineage
Multiple complete vocal takes selected/comped per Eric Valentine production approach
Less vocal layering than wall-of-sound bands — power-trio-plus-guest aesthetic
Vocal-lead democracy demonstrated within Songs for the Deaf — different tracks lead by Homme, Oliveri, or with Lanegan emphasis

performance_anti_patterns_avoided: No quantization (preserves Grohl's natural groove + hypnotic-repetition micro-variations)
No 'show-off' technical drumming inappropriate to groove-priority context (Grohl restrained for QOTSA)

stylistic_figures._note: Production gestures and figures of style — the rises, drops, transitions, climaxes, special effects that define the track's compositional identity beyond pure pitch/rhythm content. Cross-mining target.

transitions_between_sections.description: After the immediate power-trio intro establishes the riff, Homme's vocal simply enters on top — no fill, no riser, no anticipation. The riff continues unchanged underneath the vocal.
Pre-chorus builds slight density and harmonic motion, then Grohl drum fill lifts into chorus where Lanegan harmony enters. Two-step transition: density build then fill-lift.
Chorus-to-verse return marked by Lanegan harmony dropping out and returning to verse density. The subtraction of Lanegan IS the transition cue.
Bridge entry marked by brief textural/dynamic shift — possibly clean-guitar break or harmonic suspension. Shorter than typical rock-band bridges (commercial-single economy).
Final chorus arrives via riff re-engagement and Lanegan harmony returning. Compositional bookending — chorus elements recall their earlier character.
Outro accomplished via final riff statement and ending — abrupt punk-style ending or very short fade. No extended outro jam.

risers_and_builds.description: Pre-chorus builds slight density via texture addition, then Grohl drum fill lifts into chorus where Lanegan harmony enters. Power-trio riser vocabulary (no synth sweeps, no electronic risers).

cutoffs_and_filter_moves.description: Lanegan harmony dropping at chorus exit functions as a 'cutoff' of dimensional color. Performance-based subtraction rather than filter automation.
Bridge may feature clean-guitar break — distorted rhythm guitars step back for brief clean texture. Performance-based texture shift, not pedal automation.

drops_and_breakdowns.description: Bridge functions as a brief breakdown — slight density reduction before final chorus payoff. Compact compared to extended-form rock bridges; commercial-single economy.
Unlike March of the Pigs' inverted-drop structure, First It Giveth follows conventional rock-arc dynamics — chorus is the loud-payoff section, not the quiet-relief section.

climax_moments.where: final_chorus with Lanegan harmony at full deployment
climax_moments.description: The track's climax is the final chorus, where Homme's lead + Lanegan harmony + power-trio at full density create the song's emotional peak. Climax is via FULL DEPLOYMENT of all available elements rather than via volume escalation.

special_effects_and_textural_devices.description: The track's compositional engine is Homme's 'robot rock' philosophy: riff repeats near-identically across measures but never EXACTLY identically. Micro-variations (slight timing shifts, occasional different pickup notes, subtle phrasing variations) create hypnotic groove that breathes.

Mark Lanegan's whiskey-baritone harmony placed strategically in chorus only. His distinct vocal identity (Screaming Trees + solo material lineage) brings dimensional color rather than redundant doubling. Different vocal identity = different emotional color.

Guitars and bass tuned to Drop D for chromatic riff capability (heavy power chords on low D string) plus low-end weight. Documented Homme/QOTSA convention; particularly suited for stoner-rock / desert-rock contexts.
Grohl as guest drummer adapts his Nirvana-mode aggression to QOTSA's groove-conscious robot-rock context. Same drummer different mode — guest drumming as INTERPRETATION not transcription.

Marshall / Hiwatt / Mesa-Boogie tube amp palette + subtle modulation effects (chorus, occasional flange) provide desert-rock textural color without obscuring the riff. Tone serves the riff; effects are seasoning not foundation.

the_moment_people_remember.primary: The hypnotic mid-tempo riff in Drop D — Homme's robot-rock signature in compact commercial-single form. The riff's repetitive-with-micro-variations character is the track's identity.
the_moment_people_remember.secondary: Mark Lanegan's whiskey-baritone harmony entering in the chorus — the dimensional vocal color that distinguishes this track from a power-trio-only arrangement.
the_moment_people_remember.tertiary: Grohl's groove-conscious drumming — listeners who know Grohl's other work feel the ADAPTED mode (less aggressive than Nirvana, more groove-conscious than NIN With Teeth)
